description:
You are a build-pipeline engineer who has just received three raw artifact logs
    captured during the nightly builds.  
    The logs sit in the following directories that already exist on the system
    (they have been pre-populated for you):

        /home/user/builds/2023-05-01/artifacts.log
        /home/user/builds/2023-05-02/artifacts.log
        /home/user/builds/2023-05-03/artifacts.log

    1. Log format  
       Each *artifacts.log* file currently contains one record per line in this exact
       pipe-delimited format:

            artifact_name|version|size_kb|sha256|status

       • artifact_name  – lower-case, no spaces  
       • version        – semantic version string  
       • size_kb        – integer size in kilobytes  
       • sha256         – 6–64 hex characters  
       • status         – either OK or FAIL  

    2. Task goals  
       Use only standard GNU utilities that are normally present on a
       minimalist Linux image.  Most of the heavy lifting should be done with
       awk and sed working together or in a chained pipeline.

       a)  Clean CSV  
           • Create a new file
               /home/user/builds/all_artifacts_clean.csv  
           • The file **must start with exactly one header line**

                 artifact_name,version,size_kb,sha256

           • Append every record whose original status is **OK**
             (ignore FAIL lines) from **all three** log files.  
           • Change the field separator to a single comma.  
           • Sort the data lines **alphabetically by artifact_name
             and then by version (string sort)**.  
           • The final file must therefore have 7 lines total:
             1 header + 6 data lines.

       b)  Per-artifact summary  
           • Create
               /home/user/builds/artifact_summary.txt  
           • For each distinct artifact_name present in the cleaned CSV,
             output **one** line in exactly this layout  
                 artifact_name: count=<N>, total_kb=<SUM>  
             where  
               – <N>   is the number of OK entries for that artifact  
               – <SUM> is the sum of size_kb across those OK entries  
           • Lines must be sorted alphabetically by artifact_name.

       c)  Flag failed rows for re-processing  
           • For every artifacts.log file, change the STATUS field value
             FAIL → RETRY_PENDING **in-place**.  
           • Do not touch lines that already say OK.  
           • After the replacement is done, there must be **zero**
             occurrences of the literal string “|FAIL” anywhere under
             /home/user/builds.

    3. Verification rules used by automated grading  
       The grader will check, in order:

       • The file /home/user/builds/all_artifacts_clean.csv exists, is world-readable,
         contains exactly 7 lines, the first of which is the header shown above,
         and the remaining 6 lines are sorted as required.  

       • /home/user/builds/artifact_summary.txt exists, contains exactly
         the 4 correctly formatted summary lines, and no extra blank lines.  

       • grep -R --binary-files=without-match -n "|FAIL" /home/user/builds
         returns no output (i.e. all FAIL tokens were replaced).  

       • Each original artifacts.log file still contains its original lines,
         with the **sole difference** that every “|FAIL” token is now
         “|RETRY_PENDING”.

    4. Hints (do *not* reveal commands in your answer to the grader)  
       • awk’s ability to change FS/OFS is useful for delimiter conversion.  
       • A single sed -i command can handle the status replacement for all
         three logs at once if you supply multiple path arguments.  
       • Remember to redirect or pipe in a manner that never writes partial
         results to the target CSV; atomic creation makes grading easier.  

    When you are finished, both new files should be present, and the original
    logs should be updated in place.  No additional files should be created in
    /home/user other than the ones explicitly requested.
truth:
<!--
        This section contains privileged verification data.

        1. Initial state (already present when the agent starts)
    -->
    <initial_files>
        <file path="/home/user/builds/2023-05-01/artifacts.log"><![CDATA[
app-core|1.0.0|1500|abc111|OK
app-ui|1.0.0|500|def222|FAIL
lib-utils|2.1.3|300|ghi333|OK
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/builds/2023-05-02/artifacts.log"><![CDATA[
app-core|1.0.1|1520|abc444|OK
app-ui|1.0.1|520|def555|OK
lib-utils|2.1.3|300|ghi333|FAIL
plugin-auth|0.9.0|200|jkl666|OK
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/builds/2023-05-03/artifacts.log"><![CDATA[
app-core|1.0.1|1520|abc777|FAIL
plugin-auth|0.9.1|210|jkl999|OK
]]></file>
    </initial_files>

    <!--
        2. Expected final state after the agent completes the task
    -->
    <expected_final_state>
        <file path="/home/user/builds/all_artifacts_clean.csv"><![CDATA[
artifact_name,version,size_kb,sha256
app-core,1.0.0,1500,abc111
app-core,1.0.1,1520,abc444
app-ui,1.0.1,520,def555
lib-utils,2.1.3,300,ghi333
plugin-auth,0.9.0,200,jkl666
plugin-auth,0.9.1,210,jkl999
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/builds/artifact_summary.txt"><![CDATA[
app-core: count=2, total_kb=3020
app-ui: count=1, total_kb=520
lib-utils: count=1, total_kb=300
plugin-auth: count=2, total_kb=410
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/builds/2023-05-01/artifacts.log"><![CDATA[
app-core|1.0.0|1500|abc111|OK
app-ui|1.0.0|500|def222|RETRY_PENDING
lib-utils|2.1.3|300|ghi333|OK
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/builds/2023-05-02/artifacts.log"><![CDATA[
app-core|1.0.1|1520|abc444|OK
app-ui|1.0.1|520|def555|OK
lib-utils|2.1.3|300|ghi333|RETRY_PENDING
plugin-auth|0.9.0|200|jkl666|OK
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/builds/2023-05-03/artifacts.log"><![CDATA[
app-core|1.0.1|1520|abc777|RETRY_PENDING
plugin-auth|0.9.1|210|jkl999|OK
]]></file>
    </expected_final_state>
